SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (faster training and comparable BLEU; ability to learn morphology) and presents experimental evidence including Table 1 (BLEU scores, training epochs/days) and analyses in Section 5.2 (PCA projections, energy plots). Mathematical descriptions of the model (e.g. word encoder equations and Eqn. (8) for st) are plausible and consistent. However, experimental methodology lacks statistical tests and some baseline results are taken from other papers without fully controlled matching of training regimes or hardware, leaving minor uncertainty about the fairness of efficiency comparisons.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The model combines a two-RNN word encoder that learns morpheme-like weights and a hierarchical character-level decoder, which is an incremental composition of existing ideas (the paper itself cites C2W (Ling et al.), CNN encoders (Lee et al.), and hierarchical decoders). The authors claim novelty in automatically learning subword units rather than using BPE, but the approach is a non-trivial combination rather than a completely new paradigm. The paper reasonably differentiates from prior work in the related-work discussions, but the core techniques build on established methods.

CLARITY.justification: The method is described with equations, diagram references (Figures 1 and 2), and clear descriptions of the encoder/decoder stacks, and contributions are summarized in the introduction. However, the provided text contains many repetitions and formatting issues (duplicated paragraphs and fragmented sentences), which reduce readability. Notation is mostly consistent (e.g. r_t, h_t, st) but some implementation details are described only briefly, and several claims refer to figures not included in the excerpt.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental evaluation covers three language pairs (En-Fr, En-Cs, Cs-En) on WMT corpora and provides both quantitative BLEU results (Table 1) and qualitative analyses of learned morphology (Section 5.2). Important analyses are present (training efficiency, PCA plots, segmentation examples), but there are missing ablations (e.g. impact of key design choices like using tanh vs softmax for weights, choice of 120 characters) and no discussion of failure modes or statistical significance. Reproducibility is helped by many hyperparameter details but some implementation/training specifics are omitted.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses the important problem of open-vocabulary NMT and character-level modeling, showing improvements in early training and comparable final BLEU, which is of interest to the community. While the work could influence practitioners seeking efficient character-level architectures and morphology-aware encoders, it is an incremental advance among several contemporaneous approaches rather than a transformative shift. The methods may be adopted or built upon but are unlikely to alone change prevailing practices.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—neural machine translation, character-level modeling, and morphology learning—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The paper uses standard datasets (WMT En-Fr, En-Cs), evaluation metrics (BLEU), and compares to relevant prior work, addressing the right audience and employing expected methods and standards for this venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against multiple relevant baselines (bpe, C2W, CNMT, char2char, etc.) and reports BLEU as well as training epochs/days in Table 1, citing the original sources for baseline results. Comparisons are generally informative, but several baseline numbers are taken from other papers and training conditions/hardware are not fully controlled or described, which raises questions about absolute fairness of the efficiency comparisons. The paper does acknowledge when results are taken from prior work and discusses relative performance.
